flashcards.front: What is Ether (ETH) and what is its role in Ethereum?
flashcards.back: Ether is Ethereum's native cryptocurrency. It serves as: 1) A medium of exchange for transaction fees, 2) An incentive for miners/validators to secure the network, and 3) A unit of account for computational services on the network.

flashcards.front: Explain the difference between Gas Price and Gas Limit
flashcards.back: Gas Price is the amount of ETH (in Gwei) you're willing to pay per unit of gas, acting as a bid for transaction priority. Gas Limit is the maximum amount of gas you're willing to spend on a transaction, protecting against infinite loops or expensive computations.

flashcards.front: What are the two types of accounts in Ethereum and how do they differ?
flashcards.back: 1) Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs): Controlled by private keys, can hold ETH, can initiate transactions, no associated code. 2) Contract Accounts (CAs): Controlled by smart contract code, can hold ETH, execute code when triggered, cannot initiate transactions on their own.

flashcards.front: What makes the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) unique?
flashcards.back: The EVM is: 1) Decentralized - exists on every node, 2) Turing-complete - can run any computation given enough resources, 3) Deterministic - same input always produces same output, 4) Sandboxed - isolated from host system, 5) Stack-based - uses a last-in-first-out stack for operations.

flashcards.front: Why is the account-based model used in Ethereum instead of Bitcoin's UTXO model?
flashcards.back: The account-based model was chosen because: 1) It's more intuitive for developers (similar to traditional banking), 2) Enables smart contracts to maintain persistent state, 3) More efficient for complex transactions, 4) Simplifies the implementation of decentralized applications (dApps).
